revision.comment: Auto generating page. date placed far in the past so it is attributed to Immanuelle and FireflyBot sends them messages if it is ever imported to Wikipedia.

revision.text:
{{日本の寺院 |名称 = 日光院 |画像 = [[File:Tajimamyoken08.JPG|250px]] |所在地 = 兵庫県養父市八鹿町石原450 | 緯度度 = 35 | 緯度分 = 24 | 緯度秒 = 40 | 経度度 = 134 |経度分 = 42 | 経度秒 = 0 |山号 = 妙見山 |宗旨 = |宗派 = [[高野山真言宗]] |寺格 = |本尊 = [[妙見菩薩|妙見大菩薩]] |創建年 = [[敏達天皇]]の御代 |開基 = 日光慶重 |中興年 = |中興 = |正式名 = |別称 = |札所等 = [[近畿楽寿観音霊場]]5番 |文化財 = }} '''日光院'''（にっこういん）は、[[兵庫県]][[養父市]][[八鹿町]]にある[[高野山真言宗]]の寺院である。山号は妙見山。本尊は[[妙見菩薩|妙見大菩薩]]。日本三妙見の一つとして有名。
== 歴史 == 寺伝によると、[[飛鳥時代]][[敏達天皇]]の御代、日光慶重が草庵を結んで創建したと伝えられている。
== 関連項目 == * [[妙見山 (養父市・香美町)|妙見山]] * [[名草神社]] * [[相馬妙見]]（福島県） * [[八代神社|八代妙見]]（熊本県）
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.tajimamyouken.com/ 高野山真言宗 但馬妙見 日光院]
{{buddhism-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:につこういん}} [[Category:高野山真言宗]] [[Category:兵庫県の寺]] [[Category:養父市]]

{{日本の寺院 |名称 = 日光院 |画像 = [[File:Tajimamyoken08.JPG|250px]] |所在地 = 兵庫県養父市八鹿町石原450 | 緯度度 = 35 | 緯度分 = 24 | 緯度秒 = 40 | 経度度 = 134 |経度分 = 42 | 経度秒 = 0 |山号 = 妙見山 |宗旨 = |宗派 = [[高野山真言宗]] |寺格 = |本尊 = [[妙見菩薩|妙見大菩薩]] |創建年 = [[敏達天皇]]の御代 |開基 = 日光慶重 |中興年 = |中興 = |正式名 = |別称 = |札所等 = [[近畿楽寿観音霊場]]5番 |文化財 = }} '''日光院'''（にっこういん）は、[[兵庫県]][[養父市]][[八鹿町]]にある[[高野山真言宗]]の寺院である。山号は妙見山。本尊は[[妙見菩薩|妙見大菩薩]]。日本三妙見の一つとして有名。
== 歴史 == 寺伝によると、[[飛鳥時代]][[敏達天皇]]の御代、日光慶重が草庵を結んで創建したと伝えられている。

[[1485年]]（[[文明]]17年）には、[[山名政豊]]から社領寄進を受けている<ref>山名政豊妙見社領寄進状（日光院所蔵）{{harv|兵庫県立考古博物館|2010|p=38(写真掲載)}}</ref>。

revision.comment: 「地図 =Japan Hyogo」→「地図2 = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo}}に修正・追記。
revision.text:
{{日本の寺院 |名称 = 日光院 |画像 = [[File:Tajimamyoken08.JPG|270px]] |所在地 = 兵庫県養父市八鹿町石原450 | 緯度度 = 35 | 緯度分 = 24 | 緯度秒 = 40 | 経度度 = 134 |経度分 = 42 | 経度秒 = 0 |地図2 = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|lat_deg=35|lat_min=24|lat_sec=40|lon_deg=134|lon_min=42|lon_sec=0|label=日光院|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=11|position=center|width=200|float=center}} |山号 = 妙見山 |宗旨 = |宗派 = [[高野山真言宗]] |寺格 = |本尊 = [[妙見菩薩|妙見大菩薩]] |創建年 = [[敏達天皇]]の御代 |開基 = 日光慶重 |中興年 = |中興 = |正式名 = |別称 = |札所等 = [[近畿楽寿観音霊場]]5番 |文化財 = }} '''日光院'''（にっこういん）は、[[兵庫県]][[養父市]][[八鹿町]]にある[[高野山真言宗]]の寺院である。山号は妙見山。本尊は[[妙見菩薩|妙見大菩薩]]。日本三妙見の一つとして有名。
== 歴史 == 寺伝によると、[[飛鳥時代]][[敏達天皇]]の御代、日光慶重が草庵を結んで創建したと伝えられている。
[[1485年]]（[[文明 (日本)|文明]]17年）には、[[山名政豊]]から社領寄進を受けている<ref>山名政豊妙見社領寄進状（日光院所蔵）{{harv|兵庫県立考古博物館|2010|p=38(写真掲載)}}</ref>。
==参考文献== *{{Cite|和書|author=兵庫県立考古博物館|title=戦国時代の守護 山名氏の城と戦い|publisher=兵庫県立考古博物館||date=2010|ref=harv}}
==脚注== {{reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[妙見山 (養父市・香美町)|妙見山]] * [[名草神社]] * [[相馬妙見]]（福島県） * [[八代神社|八代妙見]]（熊本県）
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.tajimamyouken.com/ 高野山真言宗 但馬妙見 日光院]

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Nikko-in | image = [[File:Tajimamyoken08.JPG|270px]] | location = 450 Ishihara, Yoka-chō, Yabu-shi, Hyogo, Japan | latitude_deg = 35 | latitude_min = 24 | latitude_sec = 40 | longitude_deg= 134 | longitude_min = 42 | longitude_sec = 0 | map = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|lat_deg=35|lat_min=24|lat_sec=40|lon_deg=134|lon_min=42|lon_sec=0|label=Nikko-in|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=11|position=center|width=200|float=center}} | mountain = Myoken-san | denomination = [[Koyasan Shingon-shu]] | principal = [[Myoken Bosatsu|Myoken Daibosatsu]] | founded = Reign of Emperor Bidatsu | founder = Nikko Keiju | cultural = | pilgrimage = [[Kinki Rakuju Kannon Pilgrimage]] No. 5 }} '''Nikko-in''' (日光院) is a [[Koyasan Shingon-shu]] temple located in {{ill|Yabu|ja|養父市}}, {{ill|Hyogo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}, Japan. It is part of the Myoken-san complex and venerates [[Myoken Bosatsu]] (Myoken Daibosatsu) as its principal image. It is one of the "Three Great Myoken" temples of Japan.
== History == According to temple tradition, Nikko-in was founded during the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] (6th century CE) by the monk Nikko Keiju, who established a hermitage at the site.
In [[1485]] (17th year of the [[Bunmei era]]), the temple received a land grant from {{ill|Yamana Masatoyo|ja|山名政豊}}, demonstrating its historical significance.<ref>Yamana Masatoyo’s Myoken Shrine Land Grant (document held by Nikko-in) {{harv|Hyogo Prefectural Archaeological Museum|2010|p=38}}.</ref>
== Related sites == * [[Myoken-san (Yabu and Kami)|Myoken-san]] * [[Nagusa Shrine]] * [[Soma Myoken]] (Fukushima Prefecture) * [[Yatsushiro Shrine|Yatsushiro Myoken]] (Kumamoto Prefecture)
== References == *{{Cite book|和書|author=Hyogo Prefectural Archaeological Museum|title=The Sengoku Period Guardians: Yamana Clan Castles and Battles|date=2010|ref=harv}}
== External links == * [http://www.tajimamyouken.com/ Koyasan Shingon-shu Tajima Myoken Nikko-in] (official site)

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Nikko-in | image = [[File:Tajimamyoken08.JPG|270px]] | location = 450 Ishihara, Yoka-chō, Yabu-shi, Hyogo, Japan | latitude_deg = 35 | latitude_min = 24 | latitude_sec = 40 | longitude_deg= 134 | longitude_min = 42 | longitude_sec = 0 | map = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|lat_deg=35|lat_min=24|lat_sec=40|lon_deg=134|lon_min=42|lon_sec=0|label=Nikko-in|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=11|position=center|width=200|float=center}} | mountain = Myoken-san | denomination = [[Koyasan Shingon-shu]] | principal = [[Myoken Bosatsu|Myoken Daibosatsu]] | founded = Reign of Emperor Bidatsu | founder = Nikko Keiju | cultural = | pilgrimage = [[Kinki Rakuju Kannon Pilgrimage]] No. 5 }} '''Nikko-in''' (日光院) is a [[Koyasan Shingon-shu]] temple located in {{ill|Yabu|ja|養父市}}, {{ill|Hyogo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}, Japan. It is part of the Myoken-san complex and venerates [[Myoken Bosatsu]] (Myoken Daibosatsu) as its principal image. It is one of the "Three Great Myoken" temples of Japan.
== History == According to temple tradition, Nikko-in was founded during the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] (6th century CE) by the monk Nikko Keiju, who established a hermitage at the site.
In [[1485]] (17th year of the [[Bunmei era]]), the temple received a land grant from {{ill|Yamana Masatoyo|ja|山名政豊}}, demonstrating its historical significance.<ref>Yamana Masatoyo’s Myoken Shrine Land Grant (document held by Nikko-in) {{harv|Hyogo Prefectural Archaeological Museum|2010|p=38}}.</ref>
== Related sites == * [[Myoken-san (Yabu and Kami)|Myoken-san]] * [[Nagusa Shrine]] * [[Soma Myoken]] (Fukushima Prefecture) * [[Yatsushiro Shrine|Yatsushiro Myoken]] (Kumamoto Prefecture)

revision.text:
{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Nikko-in | image = [[File:Tajimamyoken08.JPG|270px]] | location = 450 Ishihara, Yoka-chō, Yabu-shi, Hyogo, Japan | latitude_deg = 35 | latitude_min = 24 | latitude_sec = 40 | longitude_deg= 134 | longitude_min = 42 | longitude_sec = 0 | map = {{location map|Japan Hyogo|lat_deg=35|lat_min=24|lat_sec=40|lon_deg=134|lon_min=42|lon_sec=0|label=Nikko-in|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=11|position=center|width=200|float=center}} | mountain = Myoken-san | denomination = [[Koyasan Shingon-shu]] | principal = [[Myoken Bosatsu|Myoken Daibosatsu]] | founded = Reign of Emperor Bidatsu | founder = Nikko Keiju | cultural = | pilgrimage = [[Kinki Rakuju Kannon Pilgrimage]] No. 5 }} '''Nikko-in''' (日光院) is a [[Koyasan Shingon-shu]] temple located in {{ill|Yabu|ja|養父市}}, {{ill|Hyogo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}, Japan. It is part of the Myoken-san complex and venerates [[Myoken Bosatsu]] (Myoken Daibosatsu) as its principal image. It is one of the "Three Great Myoken" temples of Japan.
== History == According to temple tradition, Nikko-in was founded during the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] (6th century CE) by the monk Nikko Keiju, who established a hermitage at the site.
In [[1485]] (17th year of the [[Bunmei era]]), the temple received a land grant from {{ill|Yamana Masatoyo|ja|山名政豊}}, demonstrating its historical significance.<ref>Yamana Masatoyo’s Myoken Shrine Land Grant (document held by Nikko-in) {{harv|Hyogo Prefectural Archaeological Museum|2010|p=38}}.</ref>
== Related sites == * [[Myoken-san (Yabu and Kami)|Myoken-san]] * [[Nagusa Shrine]] * [[Soma Myoken]] (Fukushima Prefecture) * [[Yatsushiro Shrine|Yatsushiro Myoken]] (Kumamoto Prefecture)
== References == *{{Cite book|和書|author=Hyogo Prefectural Archaeological Museum|title=The Sengoku Period Guardians: Yamana Clan Castles and Battles|date=2010|ref=harv}}
== External links == * [http://www.tajimamyouken.com/ Koyasan Shingon-shu Tajima Myoken Nikko-in] (official site)

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Nikko-in | image = [[File:Tajimamyoken08.JPG|270px]] | location = 450 Ishihara, Yoka-chō, Yabu-shi, Hyogo, Japan | latitude_deg = 35 | latitude_min = 24 | latitude_sec = 40 | longitude_deg= 134 | longitude_min = 42 | longitude_sec = 0 | map = {{location map|Japan Hyogo|lat_deg=35|lat_min=24|lat_sec=40|lon_deg=134|lon_min=42|lon_sec=0|label=Nikko-in|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=11|position=center|width=200|float=center}} | mountain = Myoken-san | denomination = [[Koyasan Shingon-shu]] | principal = [[Myoken Bosatsu|Myoken Daibosatsu]] | founded = Reign of Emperor Bidatsu | founder = Nikko Keiju | cultural = | pilgrimage = [[Kinki Rakuju Kannon Pilgrimage]] No. 5 }} '''Nikko-in''' (日光院) is a [[Koyasan Shingon-shu]] temple located in {{ill|Yabu|ja|養父市}}, {{ill|Hyogo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}, Japan. It is part of the Myoken-san complex and venerates [[Myoken Bosatsu]] (Myoken Daibosatsu) as its principal image. It is one of the "Three Great Myoken" temples of Japan.
== History == According to temple tradition, Nikko-in was founded during the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] (6th century CE) by the monk Nikko Keiju, who established a hermitage at the site.
In [[1485]] (17th year of the [[Bunmei era]]), the temple received a land grant from {{ill|Yamana Masatoyo|ja|山名政豊}}, demonstrating its historical significance.<ref>Yamana Masatoyo’s Myoken Shrine Land Grant (document held by Nikko-in) {{harv|Hyogo Prefectural Archaeological Museum|2010|p=38}}.</ref>
== Related sites == * [[Myoken-san (Yabu and Kami)|Myoken-san]] * [[Nagusa Shrine]] * [[Soma Myoken]] (Fukushima Prefecture) * [[Yatsushiro Shrine|Yatsushiro Myoken]] (Kumamoto Prefecture)
== References == *{{Cite book|和書|author=Hyogo Prefectural Archaeological Museum|title=The Sengoku Period Guardians: Yamana Clan Castles and Battles|date=2010|ref=harv}}
== External links == * [http://www.tajimamyouken.com/ Koyasan Shingon-shu Tajima Myoken Nikko-in] (official site)

In [[1485]] (17th year of the [[Bunmei era]]), the temple received a land grant from {{ill|Yamana Masatoyo|ja|山名政豊}}, demonstrating its historical significance.<ref>Yamana Masatoyo’s Myoken Shrine Land Grant (document held by Nikko-in) {{harv|Hyogo Prefectural Archaeological Museum|2010|p=38}}.</ref>
== Related sites == * [[Myoken-san (Yabu and Kami)|Myoken-san]] * [[Nagusa Shrine]] * [[Soma Myoken]] (Fukushima Prefecture) * [[Yatsushiro Shrine|Yatsushiro Myoken]] (Kumamoto Prefecture)

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Nikko-in | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Tajimamyoken08.JPG|270px]] | location = 450 Ishihara, Yoka-chō, Yabu-shi, Hyogo, Japan | latitude_deg = 35 | latitude_min = 24 | latitude_sec = 40 | longitude_deg= 134 | longitude_min = 42 | longitude_sec = 0 | map = {{location map|Japan Hyogo|lat_deg=35|lat_min=24|lat_sec=40|lon_deg=134|lon_min=42|lon_sec=0|label=Nikko-in|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=11|position=center|width=200|float=center}} | mountain = Myoken-san | denomination = [[Koyasan Shingon-shu]] | principal = [[Myoken Bosatsu|Myoken Daibosatsu]] | founded = Reign of Emperor Bidatsu | founder = Nikko Keiju | cultural = | pilgrimage = [[Kinki Rakuju Kannon Pilgrimage]] No. 5 }} '''Nikko-in''' (日光院) is a [[Koyasan Shingon-shu]] temple located in {{ill|Yabu|ja|養父市}}, {{ill|Hyogo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}, Japan. It is part of the Myoken-san complex and venerates [[Myoken Bosatsu]] (Myoken Daibosatsu) as its principal image. It is one of the "Three Great Myoken" temples of Japan.
== History == According to temple tradition, Nikko-in was founded during the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] (6th century CE) by the monk Nikko Keiju, who established a hermitage at the site.
In [[1485]] (17th year of the [[Bunmei era]]), the temple received a land grant from {{ill|Yamana Masatoyo|ja|山名政豊}}, demonstrating its historical significance.<ref>Yamana Masatoyo’s Myoken Shrine Land Grant (document held by Nikko-in) {{harv|Hyogo Prefectural Archaeological Museum|2010|p=38}}.</ref>
== Related sites == * [[Myoken-san (Yabu and Kami)|Myoken-san]] * [[Nagusa Shrine]] * [[Soma Myoken]] (Fukushima Prefecture) * [[Yatsushiro Shrine|Yatsushiro Myoken]] (Kumamoto Prefecture)
== References == *{{Cite book|和書|author=Hyogo Prefectural Archaeological Museum|title=The Sengoku Period Guardians: Yamana Clan Castles and Battles|date=2010|ref=harv}}
== External links == * [http://www.tajimamyouken.com/ Koyasan Shingon-shu Tajima Myoken Nikko-in] (official site)

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Nikko-in | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Tajimamyoken08.JPG|270px]] | location = 450 Ishihara, Yoka-chō, Yabu-shi, Hyogo, Japan | latitude_deg = 35 | latitude_min = 24 | latitude_sec = 40 | longitude_deg= 134 | longitude_min = 42 | longitude_sec = 0 | map = {{location map|Japan Hyogo|lat_deg=35|lat_min=24|lat_sec=40|lon_deg=134|lon_min=42|lon_sec=0|label=Nikko-in|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=11|position=center|width=200|float=center}} | mountain = Myoken-san | denomination = [[Koyasan Shingon-shu]] | principal = [[Myoken Bosatsu|Myoken Daibosatsu]] | founded = Reign of Emperor Bidatsu | founder = Nikko Keiju | cultural = | pilgrimage = [[Kinki Rakuju Kannon Pilgrimage]] No. 5 }} '''Nikko-in''' (日光院) is a [[Koyasan Shingon-shu]] temple located in {{ill|Yabu|ja|養父市}}, {{ill|Hyogo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}, Japan. It is part of the Myoken-san complex and venerates [[Myoken Bosatsu]] (Myoken Daibosatsu) as its principal image. It is one of the "Three Great Myoken" temples of Japan.
== History == According to temple tradition, Nikko-in was founded during the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] (6th century CE) by the monk Nikko Keiju, who established a hermitage at the site.
In [[1485]] (17th year of the [[Bunmei era]]), the temple received a land grant from {{ill|Yamana Masatoyo|ja|山名政豊}}, demonstrating its historical significance.<ref>Yamana Masatoyo’s Myoken Shrine Land Grant (document held by Nikko-in) {{harv|Hyogo Prefectural Archaeological Museum|2010|p=38}}.</ref>
== Related sites == * [[Myoken-san (Yabu and Kami)|Myoken-san]] * [[Nagusa Shrine]] * [[Soma Myoken]] (Fukushima Prefecture) * [[Yatsushiro Shrine|Yatsushiro Myoken]] (Kumamoto Prefecture)

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Nikko-in | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Tajimamyoken08.JPG|270px]] | location = 450 Ishihara, Yoka-chō, Yabu-shi, Hyogo, Japan | latitude_deg = 35 | latitude_min = 24 | latitude_sec = 40 | longitude_deg= 134 | longitude_min = 42 | longitude_sec = 0 | map = {{location map|Japan Hyogo|lat_deg=35|lat_min=24|lat_sec=40|lon_deg=134|lon_min=42|lon_sec=0|label=Nikko-in|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=11|position=center|width=200|float=center}} | mountain = Myoken-san | denomination = [[Koyasan Shingon-shu]] | principal = [[Myoken Bosatsu|Myoken Daibosatsu]] | founded = Reign of Emperor Bidatsu | founder = Nikko Keiju | cultural = | pilgrimage = [[Kinki Rakuju Kannon Pilgrimage]] No. 5 }} '''Nikko-in''' (日光院) is a [[Koyasan Shingon-shu]] temple located in {{ill|Yabu|ja|養父市}}, {{ill|Hyogo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}, Japan. It is part of the Myoken-san complex and venerates [[Myoken Bosatsu]] (Myoken Daibosatsu) as its principal image. It is one of the "Three Great Myoken" temples of Japan.
== History == According to temple tradition, Nikko-in was founded during the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] (6th century CE) by the monk Nikko Keiju, who established a hermitage at the site.
In [[1485]] (17th year of the [[Bunmei era]]), the temple received a land grant from {{ill|Yamana Masatoyo|ja|山名政豊}}, demonstrating its historical significance.<ref>Yamana Masatoyo’s Myoken Shrine Land Grant (document held by Nikko-in) {{harv|Hyogo Prefectural Archaeological Museum|2010|p=38}}.</ref>
== Related sites == * [[Myoken-san (Yabu and Kami)|Myoken-san]] * [[Nagusa Shrine]] * [[Soma Myoken]] (Fukushima Prefecture) * [[Yatsushiro Shrine|Yatsushiro Myoken]] (Kumamoto Prefecture)
== References == *{{Cite book|和書|author=Hyogo Prefectural Archaeological Museum|title=The Sengoku Period Guardians: Yamana Clan Castles and Battles|date=2010|ref=harv}}
== External links == * [http://www.tajimamyouken.com/ Koyasan Shingon-shu Tajima Myoken Nikko-in] (official site)

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Nikko-in | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Tajimamyoken08.JPG|270px]] | location = 450 Ishihara, Yoka-chō, Yabu-shi, Hyogo, Japan | latitude_deg = 35 | latitude_min = 24 | latitude_sec = 40 | longitude_deg= 134 | longitude_min = 42 | longitude_sec = 0 | map = {{location map|Japan Hyogo|lat_deg=35|lat_min=24|lat_sec=40|lon_deg=134|lon_min=42|lon_sec=0|label=Nikko-in|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=11|position=center|width=200|float=center}} | mountain = Myoken-san | denomination = [[Koyasan Shingon-shu]] | principal = [[Myoken Bosatsu|Myoken Daibosatsu]] | founded = Reign of Emperor Bidatsu | founder = Nikko Keiju | cultural = | pilgrimage = [[Kinki Rakuju Kannon Pilgrimage]] No. 5 }} '''Nikko-in''' (日光院) is a [[Koyasan Shingon-shu]] temple located in {{ill|Yabu|ja|養父市}}, {{ill|Hyogo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}, Japan. It is part of the Myoken-san complex and venerates [[Myoken Bosatsu]] (Myoken Daibosatsu) as its principal image. It is one of the "Three Great Myoken" temples of Japan.
== History == According to temple tradition, Nikko-in was founded during the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] (6th century CE) by the monk Nikko Keiju, who established a hermitage at the site.
In [[1485]] (17th year of the [[Bunmei era]]), the temple received a land grant from {{ill|Yamana Masatoyo|ja|山名政豊}}, demonstrating its historical significance.<ref>Yamana Masatoyo’s Myoken Shrine Land Grant (document held by Nikko-in) {{harv|Hyogo Prefectural Archaeological Museum|2010|p=38}}.</ref>
== Related sites == * [[Myoken-san (Yabu and Kami)|Myoken-san]] * [[Nagusa Shrine]] * [[Soma Myoken]] (Fukushima Prefecture) * [[Yatsushiro Shrine|Yatsushiro Myoken]] (Kumamoto Prefecture)
== References == *{{Cite book|和書|author=Hyogo Prefectural Archaeological Museum|title=The Sengoku Period Guardians: Yamana Clan Castles and Battles|date=2010|ref=harv}}

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Nikko-in | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Tajimamyoken08.JPG|270px]] | location = 450 Ishihara, Yoka-chō, Yabu-shi, Hyogo, Japan | latitude_deg = 35 | latitude_min = 24 | latitude_sec = 40 | longitude_deg= 134 | longitude_min = 42 | longitude_sec = 0 | map = {{location map|Japan Hyogo|lat_deg=35|lat_min=24|lat_sec=40|lon_deg=134|lon_min=42|lon_sec=0|label=Nikko-in|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=11|position=center|width=200|float=center}} | mountain = Myoken-san | denomination = [[Koyasan Shingon-shu]] | principal = [[Myoken Bosatsu|Myoken Daibosatsu]] | founded = Reign of Emperor Bidatsu | founder = Nikko Keiju | cultural = | pilgrimage = [[Kinki Rakuju Kannon Pilgrimage]] No. 5 }} '''Nikko-in''' (日光院) is a [[Koyasan Shingon-shu]] temple located in {{ill|Yabu|ja|養父市}}, {{ill|Hyogo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}, Japan. It is part of the Myoken-san complex and venerates [[Myoken Bosatsu]] (Myoken Daibosatsu) as its principal image. It is one of the "Three Great Myoken" temples of Japan.
== History == According to temple tradition, Nikko-in was founded during the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] (6th century CE) by the monk Nikko Keiju, who established a hermitage at the site.
In [[1485]] (17th year of the [[Bunmei era]]), the temple received a land grant from {{ill|Yamana Masatoyo|ja|山名政豊}}, demonstrating its historical significance.<ref>Yamana Masatoyo’s Myoken Shrine Land Grant (document held by Nikko-in) {{harv|Hyogo Prefectural Archaeological Museum|2010|p=38}}.</ref>

== History == According to temple tradition, Nikko-in was founded during the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] (6th century CE) by the monk Nikko Keiju, who established a hermitage at the site.
In [[1485]] (17th year of the [[Bunmei era]]), the temple received a land grant from {{ill|Yamana Masatoyo|ja|山名政豊}}, demonstrating its historical significance.<ref>Yamana Masatoyo’s Myoken Shrine Land Grant (document held by Nikko-in) {{harv|Hyogo Prefectural Archaeological Museum|2010|p=38}}.</ref>

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Nikko-in | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Tajimamyoken08.JPG|270px]] | location = 450 Ishihara, Yoka-chō, Yabu-shi, Hyogo, Japan | latitude_deg = 35 | latitude_min = 24 | latitude_sec = 40 | longitude_deg= 134 | longitude_min = 42 | longitude_sec = 0 | map = {{location map|Japan Hyogo|lat_deg=35|lat_min=24|lat_sec=40|lon_deg=134|lon_min=42|lon_sec=0|label=Nikko-in|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=11|position=center|width=200|float=center}} | mountain = Myoken-san | denomination = [[Koyasan Shingon-shu]] | principal = [[Myoken Bosatsu|Myoken Daibosatsu]] | founded = Reign of Emperor Bidatsu | founder = Nikko Keiju | cultural = | pilgrimage = [[Kinki Rakuju Kannon Pilgrimage]] No. 5 }} '''Nikko-in''' (日光院) is a [[Koyasan Shingon-shu]] temple located in {{ill|Yabu|ja|養父市}}, {{ill|Hyogo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}, Japan. It is part of the Myoken-san complex and venerates [[Myoken Bosatsu]] (Myoken Daibosatsu) as its principal image. It is one of the "Three Great Myoken" temples of Japan.
== History == According to temple tradition, Nikko-in was founded during the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] (6th century CE) by the monk Nikko Keiju, who established a hermitage at the site.
In [[1485]] (17th year of the [[Bunmei era]]), the temple received a land grant from {{ill|Yamana Masatoyo|ja|山名政豊}}, demonstrating its historical significance.<ref>Yamana Masatoyo’s Myoken Shrine Land Grant (document held by Nikko-in) {{harv|Hyogo Prefectural Archaeological Museum|2010|p=38}}.</ref>
== Related sites == * [[Myoken-san (Yabu and Kami)|Myoken-san]] * [[Nagusa Shrine]] * [[Soma Myoken]] (Fukushima Prefecture) * [[Yatsushiro Shrine|Yatsushiro Myoken]] (Kumamoto Prefecture)
== References == *{{Cite book|和書|author=Hyogo Prefectural Archaeological Museum|title=The Sengoku Period Guardians: Yamana Clan Castles and Battles|date=2010|ref=harv}}
== External links == * [http://www.tajimamyouken.com/ Koyasan Shingon-shu Tajima Myoken Nikko-in] (official site)

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Nikko-in | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Tajimamyoken08.JPG|270px]] | location = 450 Ishihara, Yoka-chō, Yabu-shi, Hyogo, Japan | latitude_deg = 35 | latitude_min = 24 | latitude_sec = 40 | longitude_deg= 134 | longitude_min = 42 | longitude_sec = 0 | map = {{location map|Japan Hyogo|lat_deg=35|lat_min=24|lat_sec=40|lon_deg=134|lon_min=42|lon_sec=0|label=Nikko-in|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=11|position=center|width=200|float=center}} | mountain = Myoken-san | denomination = [[Koyasan Shingon-shu]] | principal = [[Myoken Bosatsu|Myoken Daibosatsu]] | founded = Reign of Emperor Bidatsu | founder = Nikko Keiju | cultural = | pilgrimage = [[Kinki Rakuju Kannon Pilgrimage]] No. 5 }} '''Nikko-in''' (日光院) is a [[Koyasan Shingon-shu]] temple located in {{ill|Yabu|ja|養父市}}, {{ill|Hyogo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}, Japan. It is part of the Myoken-san complex and venerates [[Myoken Bosatsu]] (Myoken Daibosatsu) as its principal image. It is one of the "Three Great Myoken" temples of Japan.
== History == According to temple tradition, Nikko-in was founded during the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] (6th century CE) by the monk Nikko Keiju, who established a hermitage at the site.
In [[1485]] (17th year of the [[Bunmei era]]), the temple received a land grant from {{ill|Yamana Masatoyo|ja|山名政豊}}, demonstrating its historical significance.<ref>Yamana Masatoyo’s Myoken Shrine Land Grant (document held by Nikko-in) {{harv|Hyogo Prefectural Archaeological Museum|2010|p=38}}.</ref>
== Related sites == * [[Myoken-san (Yabu and Kami)|Myoken-san]] * [[Nagusa Shrine]] * [[Soma Myoken]] (Fukushima Prefecture) * [[Yatsushiro Shrine|Yatsushiro Myoken]] (Kumamoto Prefecture)
== References == *{{Cite book|和書|author=Hyogo Prefectural Archaeological Museum|title=The Sengoku Period Guardians: Yamana Clan Castles and Battles|date=2010|ref=harv}}
== External links == * [http://www.tajimamyouken.com/ Koyasan Shingon-shu Tajima Myoken Nikko-in] (official site)

revision.text:
{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Nikko-in | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Tajimamyoken08.JPG|270px]] | location = 450 Ishihara, Yoka-chō, Yabu-shi, Hyogo, Japan | latitude_deg = 35 | latitude_min = 24 | latitude_sec = 40 | longitude_deg= 134 | longitude_min = 42 | longitude_sec = 0 | map = {{location map|Japan Hyogo|lat_deg=35|lat_min=24|lat_sec=40|lon_deg=134|lon_min=42|lon_sec=0|label=Nikko-in|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=11|position=center|width=200|float=center}} | mountain = Myoken-san | denomination = [[Koyasan Shingon-shu]] | principal = [[Myoken Bosatsu|Myoken Daibosatsu]] | founded = Reign of Emperor Bidatsu | founder = Nikko Keiju | cultural = | pilgrimage = [[Kinki Rakuju Kannon Pilgrimage]] No. 5 }} '''Nikko-in''' (日光院) is a [[Koyasan Shingon-shu]] temple located in {{ill|Yabu|ja|養父市}}, {{ill|Hyogo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}, Japan. It is part of the Myoken-san complex and venerates [[Myoken Bosatsu]] (Myoken Daibosatsu) as its principal image. It is one of the "Three Great Myoken" temples of Japan.
== History == According to temple tradition, Nikko-in was founded during the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] (6th century CE) by the monk Nikko Keiju, who established a hermitage at the site.
In [[1485]] (17th year of the [[Bunmei era]]), the temple received a land grant from {{ill|Yamana Masatoyo|ja|山名政豊}}, demonstrating its historical significance.<ref>Yamana Masatoyo’s Myoken Shrine Land Grant (document held by Nikko-in) {{harv|Hyogo Prefectural Archaeological Museum|2010|p=38}}.</ref>
== Related sites == * [[Myoken-san (Yabu and Kami)|Myoken-san]] * [[Nagusa Shrine]] * [[Soma Myoken]] (Fukushima Prefecture) * [[Yatsushiro Shrine|Yatsushiro Myoken]] (Kumamoto Prefecture)
== References == *{{Cite book|和書|author=Hyogo Prefectural Archaeological Museum|title=The Sengoku Period Guardians: Yamana Clan Castles and Battles|date=2010|ref=harv}}
== External links == * [http://www.tajimamyouken.com/ Koyasan Shingon-shu Tajima Myoken Nikko-in] (official site)
{{Buddhism-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Nikko-in}} [[Category:Buddhist temples in Hyogo Prefecture]] [[Category:Koyasan Shingon temples]] [[Category:Yabu, Hyogo]] [[Category:History of Hyogo Prefecture]] [[de:Nikkoin]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nikkoin]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11 4]] [[Category:11]] [[en:Nikkoin]]]]

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Nikko-in | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Tajimamyoken08.JPG|270px]] | location = 450 Ishihara, Yoka-chō, Yabu-shi, Hyogo, Japan | latitude_deg = 35 | latitude_min = 24 | latitude_sec = 40 | longitude_deg= 134 | longitude_min = 42 | longitude_sec = 0 | map = {{location map|Japan Hyogo|lat_deg=35|lat_min=24|lat_sec=40|lon_deg=134|lon_min=42|lon_sec=0|label=Nikko-in|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=11|position=center|width=200|float=center}} | mountain = Myoken-san | denomination = [[Koyasan Shingon-shu]] | principal = [[Myoken Bosatsu|Myoken Daibosatsu]] | founded = Reign of Emperor Bidatsu | founder = Nikko Keiju | cultural = | pilgrimage = [[Kinki Rakuju Kannon Pilgrimage]] No. 5 }} '''Nikko-in''' (日光院) is a [[Koyasan Shingon-shu]] temple located in {{ill|Yabu|ja|養父市}}, {{ill|Hyogo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}, Japan. It is part of the Myoken-san complex and venerates [[Myoken Bosatsu]] (Myoken Daibosatsu) as its principal image. It is one of the "Three Great Myoken" temples of Japan.
== History == According to temple tradition, Nikko-in was founded during the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] (6th century CE) by the monk Nikko Keiju, who established a hermitage at the site.
In [[1485]] (17th year of the [[Bunmei era]]), the temple received a land grant from {{ill|Yamana Masatoyo|ja|山名政豊}}, demonstrating its historical significance.<ref>Yamana Masatoyo’s Myoken Shrine Land Grant (document held by Nikko-in) {{harv|Hyogo Prefectural Archaeological Museum|2010|p=38}}.</ref>

revision.text:
{{日本の寺院 |名称 = 日光院 |画像 = [[File:Tajimamyoken08.JPG|270px]] |所在地 = 兵庫県養父市八鹿町石原450 | 緯度度 = 35 | 緯度分 = 24 | 緯度秒 = 40 | 経度度 = 134 |経度分 = 42 | 経度秒 = 0 |地図2 = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|lat_deg=35|lat_min=24|lat_sec=40|lon_deg=134|lon_min=42|lon_sec=0|label=日光院|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=11|position=center|width=200|float=center}} |山号 = 妙見山 |宗旨 = |宗派 = [[高野山真言宗]] |寺格 = |本尊 = [[妙見菩薩|妙見大菩薩]] |創建年 = [[敏達天皇]]の御代 |開基 = 日光慶重 |中興年 = |中興 = |正式名 = |別称 = |札所等 = [[近畿楽寿観音霊場]]5番 |文化財 = }} '''日光院'''（にっこういん）は、[[兵庫県]][[養父市]][[八鹿町]]にある[[高野山真言宗]]の寺院である。山号は妙見山。本尊は[[妙見菩薩|妙見大菩薩]]。日本三妙見の一つとして有名。
== 歴史 == 寺伝によると、[[飛鳥時代]][[敏達天皇]]の御代、日光慶重が草庵を結んで創建したと伝えられている。
[[1485年]]（[[文明 (日本)|文明]]17年）には、[[山名政豊]]から社領寄進を受けている<ref>山名政豊妙見社領寄進状（日光院所蔵）{{harv|兵庫県立考古博物館|2010|p=38(写真掲載)}}</ref>。
==参考文献== *{{Cite|和書|author=兵庫県立考古博物館|title=戦国時代の守護 山名氏の城と戦い|publisher=兵庫県立考古博物館||date=2010|ref=harv}}
==脚注== {{reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[妙見山 (養父市・香美町)|妙見山]] * [[名草神社]] * [[相馬妙見]]（福島県） * [[八代神社|八代妙見]]（熊本県）
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.tajimamyouken.com/ 高野山真言宗 但馬妙見 日光院]
{{buddhism-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:につこういん}} [[Category:兵庫県の寺]] [[Category:高野山真言宗の寺院]] [[Category:養父市の建築物]] [[Category:養父市の歴史]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:日光院]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11504904]] Wikidata name: Nikkō-in [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Nikko-in | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Tajimamyoken08.JPG | location = 450 Ishihara, Yoka-chō, Yabu-shi, Hyōgo, Japan | latitude_deg = 35 | latitude_min = 24 | latitude_sec = 40 | longitude_deg= 134 | longitude_min = 42 | longitude_sec = 0 | map = {{location map|Japan Hyōgo|lat_deg=35|lat_min=24|lat_sec=40|lon_deg=134|lon_min=42|lon_sec=0|label=Nikko-in|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=11|position=center|width=200|float=center}} | mountain = Myoken-san | denomination = [[:en:Koyasan Shingon-shu|Koyasan Shingon-shu]] | principal = [[:en:Myōken|Myoken Daibosatsu]] | founded = Reign of Emperor Bidatsu | founder = Nikko Keiju | cultural = | pilgrimage = [[Kinki Rakuju Kannon Pilgrimage]] No. 5 | prefecture= {{ill|Yabu|qid=Q869014}} | coordinates= {{Coord|35.411111|N|134.7|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity= {{ill|Myōken|qid=Q10960623}} }} '''Nikko-in''' (日光院) is a [[:en:Koyasan Shingon-shu|Koyasan Shingon-shu]] temple located in [[:en:Yabu, Hyōgo|Yabu]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]], Japan. It is part of the Myoken-san complex and venerates [[:en:Myōken|Myōken]] (Myoken Daibosatsu) as its principal image. It is one of the "Three Great Myoken" temples of Japan.
== History == According to temple tradition, Nikko-in was founded during the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] (6th century CE) by the monk Nikko Keiju, who established a hermitage at the site.
In [[:en:1485|1485]] (17th year of the [[Bunmei era]]), the temple received a land grant from {{ill|Yamana Masatoyo|ja|山名政豊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamana Masatoyo|qq=|1=Masatoyo Yamana|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11467585}}, demonstrating its historical significance.<ref>Yamana Masatoyo’s Myoken Shrine Land Grant (document held by Nikko-in) {{harv|Hyōgo Prefectural Archaeological Museum|2010|p=38}}.</ref>
== Related sites == * [[Myoken-san (Yabu and Kami)|Myoken-san]] * [[Nagusa Shrine]] * [[Soma Myoken]] (Fukushima Prefecture) * [[Yatsushiro Shrine|Yatsushiro Myoken]] (Kumamoto Prefecture)

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:日光院]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11504904]] Wikidata name: Nikkō-in [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Nikko-in | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Tajimamyoken08.JPG | location = 450 Ishihara, Yoka-chō, Yabu-shi, Hyōgo, Japan | latitude_deg = 35 | latitude_min = 24 | latitude_sec = 40 | longitude_deg= 134 | longitude_min = 42 | longitude_sec = 0 | map = {{location map|Japan Hyōgo|lat_deg=35|lat_min=24|lat_sec=40|lon_deg=134|lon_min=42|lon_sec=0|label=Nikko-in|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=11|position=center|width=200|float=center}} | mountain = Myoken-san | denomination = [[:en:Koyasan Shingon-shu|Koyasan Shingon-shu]] | principal = [[:en:Myōken|Myoken Daibosatsu]] | founded = Reign of Emperor Bidatsu | founder = Nikko Keiju | cultural = | pilgrimage = [[Kinki Rakuju Kannon Pilgrimage]] No. 5 | prefecture= {{ill|Yabu|WD=Q869014}} | coordinates= {{Coord|35.411111|N|134.7|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity= {{ill|Myōken|WD=Q10960623}} }} '''Nikko-in''' (日光院) is a [[:en:Koyasan Shingon-shu|Koyasan Shingon-shu]] temple located in [[:en:Yabu, Hyōgo|Yabu]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]], Japan. It is part of the Myoken-san complex and venerates [[:en:Myōken|Myōken]] (Myoken Daibosatsu) as its principal image. It is one of the "Three Great Myoken" temples of Japan.
== History == According to temple tradition, Nikko-in was founded during the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] (6th century CE) by the monk Nikko Keiju, who established a hermitage at the site.
In [[:en:1485|1485]] (17th year of the [[Bunmei era]]), the temple received a land grant from {{ill|Yamana Masatoyo|ja|山名政豊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamana Masatoyo|qq=|1=Masatoyo Yamana|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11467585}}, demonstrating its historical significance.<ref>Yamana Masatoyo’s Myoken Shrine Land Grant (document held by Nikko-in) {{harv|Hyōgo Prefectural Archaeological Museum|2010|p=38}}.</ref>
== Related sites == * [[Myoken-san (Yabu and Kami)|Myoken-san]] * [[Nagusa Shrine]] * [[Soma Myoken]] (Fukushima Prefecture) * [[Yatsushiro Shrine|Yatsushiro Myoken]] (Kumamoto Prefecture)
== References == {{reflist}} *{{Cite book|和書|author=Hyōgo Prefectural Archaeological Museum|title=The Sengoku Period Guardians: Yamana Clan Castles and Battles|date=2010|ref=harv}}
== External links == * [http://www.tajimamyouken.com/ Koyasan Shingon-shu Tajima Myoken Nikko-in] (official site)
{{Buddhism-stub}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:日光院]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11504904]] Wikidata name: Nikkō-in [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Nikko-in | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Tajimamyoken08.JPG | location = 450 Ishihara, Yoka-chō, Yabu-shi, Hyōgo, Japan | latitude_deg = 35 | latitude_min = 24 | latitude_sec = 40 | longitude_deg= 134 | longitude_min = 42 | longitude_sec = 0 | map = {{location map|Japan Hyōgo|lat_deg=35|lat_min=24|lat_sec=40|lon_deg=134|lon_min=42|lon_sec=0|label=Nikko-in|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=11|position=center|width=200|float=center}} | mountain = Myoken-san | denomination = [[:en:Koyasan Shingon-shu|Koyasan Shingon-shu]] | principal = [[:en:Myōken|Myoken Daibosatsu]] | founded = Reign of Emperor Bidatsu | founder = Nikko Keiju | cultural = | pilgrimage = [[Kinki Rakuju Kannon Pilgrimage]] No. 5 | prefecture= {{ill|Yabu|WD=Q869014}} | coordinates= {{Coord|35.411111|N|134.7|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity= {{ill|Myōken|WD=Q10960623}} }} '''Nikko-in''' (日光院) is a [[:en:Koyasan Shingon-shu|Koyasan Shingon-shu]] temple located in [[:en:Yabu, Hyōgo|Yabu]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]], Japan. It is part of the Myoken-san complex and venerates [[:en:Myōken|Myōken]] (Myoken Daibosatsu) as its principal image. It is one of the "Three Great Myoken" temples of Japan.
== History == According to temple tradition, Nikko-in was founded during the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] (6th century CE) by the monk Nikko Keiju, who established a hermitage at the site.
In [[:en:1485|1485]] (17th year of the [[Bunmei era]]), the temple received a land grant from {{ill|Yamana Masatoyo|ja|山名政豊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamana Masatoyo|1=Masatoyo Yamana|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11467585}}, demonstrating its historical significance.<ref>Yamana Masatoyo’s Myoken Shrine Land Grant (document held by Nikko-in) {{harv|Hyōgo Prefectural Archaeological Museum|2010|p=38}}.</ref>
== Related sites == * [[Myoken-san (Yabu and Kami)|Myoken-san]] * [[Nagusa Shrine]] * [[Soma Myoken]] (Fukushima Prefecture) * [[Yatsushiro Shrine|Yatsushiro Myoken]] (Kumamoto Prefecture)
== References == {{reflist}} *{{Cite book|和書|author=Hyōgo Prefectural Archaeological Museum|title=The Sengoku Period Guardians: Yamana Clan Castles and Battles|date=2010|ref=harv}}
== External links == * [http://www.tajimamyouken.com/ Koyasan Shingon-shu Tajima Myoken Nikko-in] (official site)
{{Buddhism-stub}}
{{Myōken Faith}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:日光院]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:日光院]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:日光院]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11504904]] Wikidata name: Nikkō-in [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Nikko-in | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Tajimamyoken08.JPG | location = 450 Ishihara, Yoka-chō, Yabu-shi, Hyōgo, Japan | latitude_deg = 35 | latitude_min = 24 | latitude_sec = 40 | longitude_deg= 134 | longitude_min = 42 | longitude_sec = 0 | map = {{location map|Japan Hyōgo|lat_deg=35|lat_min=24|lat_sec=40|lon_deg=134|lon_min=42|lon_sec=0|label=Nikko-in|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=11|position=center|width=200|float=center}} | mountain = Myoken-san | denomination = [[:en:Koyasan Shingon-shu|Koyasan Shingon-shu]] | principal = [[:en:Myōken|Myoken Daibosatsu]] | founded = Reign of Emperor Bidatsu | founder = Nikko Keiju | cultural = | pilgrimage = [[Kinki Rakuju Kannon Pilgrimage]] No. 5 | prefecture= {{ill|Yabu|WD=Q869014}} | coordinates= {{Coord|35.411111|N|134.7|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity= {{ill|Myōken|WD=Q10960623}} }} '''Nikko-in''' (日光院) is a [[:en:Koyasan Shingon-shu|Koyasan Shingon-shu]] temple located in [[:en:Yabu, Hyōgo|Yabu]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]], Japan. It is part of the Myoken-san complex and venerates [[:en:Myōken|Myōken]] (Myoken Daibosatsu) as its principal image. It is one of the "Three Great Myoken" temples of Japan.
== History == According to temple tradition, Nikko-in was founded during the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] (6th century CE) by the monk Nikko Keiju, who established a hermitage at the site.
In [[:en:1485|1485]] (17th year of the [[Bunmei era]]), the temple received a land grant from {{ill|Yamana Masatoyo|ja|山名政豊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamana Masatoyo|1=Masatoyo Yamana|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11467585}}, demonstrating its historical significance.<ref>Yamana Masatoyo’s Myoken Shrine Land Grant (document held by Nikko-in) {{harv|Hyōgo Prefectural Archaeological Museum|2010|p=38}}.</ref>
== Related sites == * [[Myoken-san (Yabu and Kami)|Myoken-san]] * [[Nagusa Shrine]] * [[Soma Myoken]] (Fukushima Prefecture) * [[Yatsushiro Shrine|Yatsushiro Myoken]] (Kumamoto Prefecture)
== References == {{reflist}} *{{Cite book|和書|author=Hyōgo Prefectural Archaeological Museum|title=The Sengoku Period Guardians: Yamana Clan Castles and Battles|date=2010|ref=harv}}

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Nikko-in | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Tajimamyoken08.JPG | location = 450 Ishihara, Yoka-chō, Yabu-shi, Hyōgo, Japan | latitude_deg = 35 | latitude_min = 24 | latitude_sec = 40 | longitude_deg= 134 | longitude_min = 42 | longitude_sec = 0 | map = {{location map|Japan Hyōgo|lat_deg=35|lat_min=24|lat_sec=40|lon_deg=134|lon_min=42|lon_sec=0|label=Nikko-in|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=11|position=center|width=200|float=center}} | mountain = Myoken-san | denomination = [[:en:Koyasan Shingon-shu|Koyasan Shingon-shu]] | principal = {{ill|Myōken|en|Myōken|WD=Q10960623|lt=Myoken Daibosatsu}} | founded = Reign of Emperor Bidatsu | founder = Nikko Keiju | cultural = | pilgrimage = [[Kinki Rakuju Kannon Pilgrimage]] No. 5 | prefecture= {{ill|Yabu|WD=Q869014}} | coordinates= {{Coord|35.411111|N|134.7|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity= {{ill|Myōken|WD=Q10960623}} }} '''Nikko-in''' (日光院) is a [[:en:Koyasan Shingon-shu|Koyasan Shingon-shu]] temple located in {{ill|Yabu, Hyōgo|en|Yabu, Hyōgo|WD=Q869014|lt=Yabu}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture}}, Japan. It is part of the Myoken-san complex and venerates {{ill|Myōken|en|Myōken|WD=Q10960623|lt=Myōken}} (Myoken Daibosatsu) as its principal image. It is one of the "Three Great Myoken" temples of Japan.
== History == According to temple tradition, Nikko-in was founded during the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] (6th century CE) by the monk Nikko Keiju, who established a hermitage at the site.
In {{ill|1485|en|1485|WD=Q6644|lt=1485}} (17th year of the [[Bunmei era]]), the temple received a land grant from {{ill|Yamana Masatoyo|ja|山名政豊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamana Masatoyo|1=Masatoyo Yamana|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11467585}}, demonstrating its historical significance.<ref>Yamana Masatoyo’s Myoken Shrine Land Grant (document held by Nikko-in) {{harv|Hyōgo Prefectural Archaeological Museum|2010|p=38}}.</ref>
== Related sites == * [[Myoken-san (Yabu and Kami)|Myoken-san]] * [[Nagusa Shrine]] * [[Soma Myoken]] (Fukushima Prefecture) * [[Yatsushiro Shrine|Yatsushiro Myoken]] (Kumamoto Prefecture)
== References == {{reflist}} *{{Cite book|和書|author=Hyōgo Prefectural Archaeological Museum|title=The Sengoku Period Guardians: Yamana Clan Castles and Battles|date=2010|ref=harv}}
== External links == * [http://www.tajimamyouken.com/ Koyasan Shingon-shu Tajima Myoken Nikko-in] (official site)
{{Buddhism-stub}}
{{Myōken Faith}}
{{translated page|ja|日光院|version=101075995|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[de:Nikkoin]]
